instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
ULIP
What is ULIP?
What do you get when you combine insurance and market-linked investments?
You get a ULIP.
Short for Unit Linked Insurance Plans, ULIPs are new-age life insurance policies that offer you the dual benefits of insurance coverage and market-linked returns. With ULIPs, you can create wealth by investing in market-linked instruments, all the while enjoying protection through life insurance coverage. In the case of premature death, the plan pays a guaranteed death benefit that helps your family deal with financial loss.
What Is a ULIP Plan?
A ULIP plan is a life insurance policy wherein your premiums are invested in the market in a fund of your choice. You can invest in different types of ULIP plans like child ULIPs, endowment ULIPs, pension ULIPs, whole life ULIPs, etc. and create a suitable corpus for your financial goals. The plan gives you insurance protection and also allows you to earn inflation-adjusted returns based on market performance.
ULIP Plan: Benefits You Should Know
Market Linked Returns
ULIPs are the only life insurance plans that allow you to invest in the capital markets and get returns linked to their performance. The capital market has the potential to offer attractive and exponential returns on your investments. You can harness this potential with the help of ULIPs.
Moreover, the returns offered under ULIPs are inflation-adjusted. This means that the returns grow in tandem with inflation so that the corpus that you get is sufficient to cater to your financial goals.
Dual Benefit of Investment and Insurance
Besides allowing attractive returns, ULIPs also give you the security of insurance coverage. So, while you invest money in ULIPs to create a corpus for your financial needs, you can be assured that your family’s financial needs will be taken care of in your absence.
Flexibility
ULIP plans are a flexible investment option. It offers flexibility to-
Switch between investment funds based on market outlook
Make partial withdrawals
Do a top-up to invest an additional sum of money
Do a premium redirection as per the change of financial goals
Level Paying Premiums
Under ULIPs, the premium does not change after the policy starts. You pay a level premium throughout the premium payment tenure. This helps you plan for your investment beforehand without any surprises. Moreover, you can also pay a single premium if you want to invest once and get covered for a longer period of time.
Even Distribution of Charges
The charges associated with ULIPs are not deducted once. They are distributed over the policy tenure so that you don’t feel the pinch at once. Moreover, it allows a maximum portion of the premium to be invested so that your investments gain maximum exposure for better return potential.
Tax Benefits
You can save taxes with ULIPs. Here’s how –
Premium paid is allowed as a deduction under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961
The death benefit is always tax-free
Partial withdrawals are not taxed, subject to satisfaction of conditions mentioned under Section 10(10D) of Income Tax Act.
Surrender value is tax-free under Section 10(10D), subject to satisfaction of conditions mentioned therein.  Also note that if policy is surrendered before 5 years from the date of commencement of policy, any deduction claimed towards premium under Section 80C will be treated as Income in the year of surrender of such ULIP policy.
The maturity benefit is also tax-free under Section 10(10D) provided the premium paid was up to Rs.2.5 lakhs, and it was not more than 10% of the sum insured.
High Liquidity
ULIPs are liquid plans as they allow you to make partial withdrawals after the lock-in period is over. Such withdrawals are tax-free and allow you to meet your financial needs without surrendering the policy.
How Do ULIPs Work?
ULIPs are different from other life insurance policies. Under these plans, you know exactly how much premium is being invested, how, and the returns that you are earning every year. So, let’s see how ULIPs work.
When you buy a ULIP, you pay a premium for the plan. You also choose the sum assured (as a multiple of the premium), the policy term, the premium payment term and the fund into which you want to invest.
There are three main types of investment funds available under ULIPs. You can choose one or more of these funds to invest in your premium.
If you have a high-risk appetite, you can choose to invest in equity funds. On the other hand, if you aren't willing to take too much of a risk, you can put your money in a debt fund. To enjoy the benefits of returns without high risks, people also prefer investing in a combination of both equity and debt funds.
Once the selection is made, the insurance company will deduct the relevant charges from the premium.
The premium, net of charges, also called allocated premium, is invested in the selected fund(s)
The relevant charges are deducted as and when they accrue
The invested premium keeps growing based on the market movements and the corresponding fund performance
The fund value is the value of your invested premium, with returns
The death benefit is guaranteed to be higher than the sum assured or the fund value
On maturity, however, you receive the fund value. ULIPs also allow the settlement option on maturity. Under this option, you can withdraw your maturity amount in instalments over five years rather than in one lump sum
During the plan term, ULIPs allow top-ups, switching, partial withdrawals and premium redirections so that you can manage your investment easily.
How to Choose the Ideal ULIP Plan suited for you?
As mentioned earlier, there are different types of ULIP plans available in the market. So, how do you choose the Ideal plan?
The answer is simple, through a fact-finding analysis.
To choose the right plan, do the following –
Evaluate your financial needs and goals. Then, depending on them, choose a suitable ULIP. For instance, if you want to plan for your child’s future, you can opt for child ULIPs. On the other hand, if you want to plan for your retirement, go for pension ULIPs.
Assess your coverage needs. After selecting the type of ULIP, choose the sum assured that would be optimal to cover your financial needs.
Estimate the premium. Decide on the premium that you can comfortably pay towards the policy to create the desired corpus. Also, assess for how long you can pay the premium.
Choose the term of the policy based on your financial goals
Choose the fund(s) depending on your risk profile and investment strategy
Go for a long-term plan for maximising your returns and minimising market volatility
Check the charges. Opt for plans that have lower charges so that a maximum part of your premium is invested
Check the plan benefits to know exactly what you would receive on death or maturity
Are ULIPs Good for Me?
Since ULIPs do not guarantee returns and are exposed to market-related risks, many of you might wonder if they are good for you or not. To find the answer to this question, ask yourself the following questions instead –
Why Are You Investing?
ULIPs have been designed to help you meet your financial goals. So, before you invest in a goal-oriented plan, make sure you outline your financial goals and identify the plan that will help you achieve your life goals.
What Kind of Charges Do I Need to Pay?
Since ULIPs offer individuals both insurance and investment, there are charges that come with investing in such a plan. Before you decide to put your money into a ULIP, make sure you educate yourself on the kind of charges that each insurance provider levies on their ULIPs.
How Do I Decide My Insurance Cover?
It's important to remember that along with investment opportunities, ULIPs also offer life cover. They are first and foremost insurance products, so you need to choose adequate life cover for yourself to safeguard your loved one's future. If you opt for the maximum sum assured offered by an insurer as per the terms & conditions of the plan, your family will be provided with adequate financial backing in the event that something happens to you.
Now that you have a better understanding of what is a ULIP plan, you can safely decide how and where you need to invest your money. If you're ready to safeguard your future, browse through different ULIP Plans that suit your needs.
Who Should Invest in ULIP Policy?
People with Varying Risk Appetite
ULIPs offer different fund options as per the risk appetite of the individual. There is a choice of funds for all types of individuals- from high-risk takers to risk-averse individuals that can help achieve their life goals.
People who Want to Closely Monitor their Investments:
A Unit Linked Insurance Plan allows you to closely monitor your investment portfolio. ULIPs also allow you to switch your funds to re-adjust the capital allocation with varying risk profiles.
People with Medium to Long Term Investment Horizon
ULIP is an ideal investment option for you if you are ready to stay invested for a relatively long period.
Investors across all Stages of Life
ULIPs offer different plans which can be selected by an investor depending upon the life stage he is currently in. ULIPs can protect your family against financial liabilities now depending on your life goal.
Types of ULIP Plans
As mentioned earlier, there are different types of ULIP plans. Let’s have a look at these types –
Types of ULIPs based on the plan structure
Depending on the type of plan and its benefit structure is, ULIPs are categorised into the following types –
Endowment ULIPs
These are the most popular ULIP plans that help you maximise your wealth and grow a corpus for any financial goal. The plans are simple ULIPs with a guaranteed benefit on death and the fund value payable on maturity.
Child ULIPs
Child ULIPs are specifically designed to create a secured corpus for a child’s future. Child ULIPs can be bought only by parents who have a minor child, usually aged 10 and below. The parent is the policyholder, and also the life insured in most cases, and the child is the beneficiary. Some plans cover the life of the child while the parent is the policyholder.
The plans usually come with an inbuilt premium waiver benefit. This means that if the parent, who is the policyholder, dies during the policy tenure, the policy would remain unaffected. The future premiums are waived off or cancelled. The insurance company pays the premium on the parent’s behalf, and the plan continues till maturity. If the parent is life insured, a death benefit is also paid at the time of the parent’s death. Thereafter, the plan continues. If, on the other hand, the child is insured, no death benefit is paid on the parent’s death. The premium is simply waived off, and the plan continues till maturity. On maturity, the maturity benefit is paid, after which the plan terminates.
Child plans, thus, ensure the creation of a corpus for the child’s future, whether or not the parent is alive.
Pension ULIPs
Pension ULIPs are specifically designed plans meant to create a retirement corpus. You pay premiums during the policy tenure which get accumulated into a retirement corpus. In the case of death, a death benefit is paid. However, when the plan matures, you can withdraw up to 60% of the accumulated corpus in cash. This is called the commutation of pension. The remaining corpus would be used to pay annuities, either immediately or after a chosen tenure.
Whole-life ULIPs
Whole-life ULIPs are like endowment ULIPs with the only difference being that they run till 99 or 100 years of age. The premium payment, however, is limited up to a specific age, and you can enjoy lifelong coverage.
Types of ULIPs Categorized by Death Benefits
There are two main types of ULIPs based on the death benefit that they pay. These are as follows –
Basic ULIPs
These ULIPs pay higher the sum assured or the fund value on death during the policy tenure.
ULIPs with an enhanced death benefit
Under these ULIPs, the death benefit is the sum assured and the fund value, together. Thus, you get an enhanced death benefit under these plans. However, remember that such plans would also incur higher mortality charges since the coverage is higher.
Fund Option Under ULIPs
As mentioned earlier, there are three primary types of investment funds under ULIPs. These are as follows –
Equity funds
Equity funds are those that invest a major portion of their portfolio into equity stocks and related securities. These funds have a high equity exposure and are, thus, high-risk investment options with a potential for high returns too.
Debt funds
Debt funds invest a major proportion of their portfolio into debt instruments. Debt instruments are those that carry a fixed rate of interest. As such, debt instruments are not volatile and do not suffer a loss if the market falls.
Debt funds, thus, are low-risk funds that offer low yet stable returns.
Hybrid or balanced funds
Hybrid funds or balanced funds are those that combine both equity and debt. The portfolio of these funds invests in equity and debt instruments. The high-risk nature of equity is stabilised by the low-risk debt component. on the other hand, the low returns offered by debt are alleviated due to the high returns offered by equity.  Balanced funds, thus, offer investors moderate returns at moderate risks.
Charges under ULIPs
There are different types of charges that are levied under ULIP plans. These charges are as follows –
Premium allocation charge
This is an upfront charge which is deducted from the premium annually. The charge is levied to account for the expenses associated with issuing the policy.
Policy administration charge
The charge is levied monthly for administering and servicing your policy.
Fund management charge
This charge is applied to pay for the fund manager who manages your fund. Fund management charge depends on the type of fund that you choose. It is higher for equity funds and lower for debt funds.
Mortality charge
A mortality charge is a charge deducted to cover the cost of risk coverage that the plan provides. In simple terms, it is the cost of providing life insurance coverage on the plan. The mortality charge depends on your age and sum assured. Moreover, it is levied till the sum assured is lower than the fund value. Once the sum assured exceeds the fund value, the mortality charge is not applicable.
Surrender or discontinuance charges
This charge is levied if you surrender your policy within the lock-in period of five years. The charge is higher in the initial years and reduces as the term proceeds.
Rider charge
If you choose a rider with your ULIP, the rider charge would be deducted for allowing you the rider coverage
Miscellaneous charge
The miscellaneous charge includes other charges associated with the policy like the charge of issuing a duplicate policy document, any other type of servicing charge, etc.
Important ULIP terms that you should know
When investing in ULIPs, you should understand the technical aspects of the plan. As such, to help you, here are some important ULIP terms that you should know about –
Lock-in
A ULIP policy has a lock-in period which means that the money that you invest stays invested for the period. The lock-in period is five years, during which you cannot make withdrawals or surrender the policy.
Switching
Switching means changing between the investment funds. You can change the selected funds as and when you need them through switching. For instance, if you invest in an equity fund, and then you want to move your investment to a debt fund, you can switch from equity to debt.
ULIP plans allow free switching facility up to a specified number of times. Once you exceed the free switches, charges might apply.
Partial withdrawals
Partial withdrawals mean withdrawing funds from your ULIP during the policy tenure. These withdrawals are available after the lock-in period. You can withdraw partially, up to specified limits to get funds for your financial needs.
Premium redirection
Premium redirection means redirecting subsequent premiums to another investment fund than what you chose to invest in. For instance, you choose equity initially and then redirect your premium to debt fund from the next year.
Top-up premium
A top-up premium is an additional premium that you can pay towards a ULIP plan over and above the actual premium that you are paying. Top-up helps you invest more and also enjoy an additional sum assured.
Settlement Option
On maturity, you can choose to take the maturity benefit in instalments rather than in one lump sum. When you do so, it is called the settlement option. You can withdraw in equal instalments over a period of five years. This helps you stay invested for longer to enjoy better returns.
What is ULIP Calculator?
ULIPs show the utilisation of your premium down to the rupee. You can check the charges and the expected returns using the ULIP calculator. A calculator is an online tool that tells you the expected corpus you can create from the plan after considering the following factors –
The amount of premium that you invest
Your age (for determining the mortality charge)
The sum assured chosen (for determining the mortality charge)
The policy term
The premium paying term and frequency
Additional riders if chosen (for determining the rider charge)
The applicable charges under the plan
The death benefit
The maturity benefit at assured rates of 6% and 10% compounded every year
You get a detailed illustration of the policy that you choose that mentions the aforementioned points so that you know how the plan works.
How Is ULIP Plan Structured?
ULIP plans are structured along the lines of mutual funds. The premium is collected from different policyholders and pooled into a collective corpus. The corpus is created for each type of fund that the ULIP plan offers.
After the corpus is created, it is allocated to different types of market-linked instruments depending on the objective of the fund. For instance, in the case of equity funds, the corpus is invested in the equity stocks of different companies and other equity securities.
The portfolio of every fund is managed by experienced fund managers who try and maximise returns and minimise risks.
Thereafter, depending on how the securities perform, the fund grows or falls. As an investor, you get returns if the fund grows or lose if the fund falls.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
What is the meaning of ULIP? How does the plan work?
ULIP is an investment-oriented life insurance policy which provides insurance coverage and investment returns.
The premium that you pay towards the ULIP is invested in a fund that you choose. Applicable charges are deducted from the premium when you invest and also recurringly as the plan is managed.
If your selected fund grows, you can earn returns on your investment.
In the case of death, the plan usually pays higher than the sum assured or the available fund value. On maturity, however, the available fund value is paid.
ULIPs have a lock-in period of five years. After this period is over, you can make partial withdrawals. Switching and premium redirection are also allowed, wherein you can change your investment funds.
What are the things that should be remembered while investing in ULIPs?
You should remember the following when investing in a ULIP –
There would be different types of charges associated with the plan
The returns would not be guaranteed. They would depend on the market performance
There would be a lock-in period of five years
If you have invested in equity funds and the market is falling, your returns might be negative.
If you invest more than Rs.2.5 lakhs in new ULIP policy/polcies which are issued  on or after 1 February 2021, the maturity benefit would be taxable as Capital Gain.
Do ULIPs make a good investment?
Yes, ULIP is a good investment because of the following reasons –
It allows dual benefits of insurance and investment
It allows flexibility in the form of top-ups, switching, premium redirection and partial withdrawals
You can choose from the different types of ULIPs to find a suitable plan depending on your financial goals
You can invest in the market and earn attractive and inflation-adjusted returns
You can enjoy tax benefits on your investment and also on the returns.
How much premium is allocated towards the funds to buy units?
The premium, net of charges, is used to purchase units. The charges include the premium allocation charge, which is deducted upfront. Thereafter, other charges are deducted on a monthly or daily basis.
The charge structure depends on the insurance company and usually amounts to up to 5%. Thus, 95% of the premium that you pay would be invested in the fund that you choose.
Is it possible to cancel the policy and get a refund after buying it?
Yes, there’s a free-look period available under the ULIP policy which is for 15 days or 30 days. During this period, you can cancel the plan if you are not satisfied with it. Upon such cancellation, the premium paid would be refunded back after deducting minor charges \incurred in issuing the policy and for the risk coverage granted before the cancellation.
What is the lock-in tenure under ULIP?
The minimum lock-in period for ULIP is five years.
What is the meaning of NAV in ULIPs?
The Net Asset Value (NAV) is the per unit cost of a fund. In simpler terms, the funds of a ULIP plan invest in different market-linked securities. The aggregate value of such securities divided by the number of securities gives the NAV. When you pay the premium, the number of units that you get depends on the NAV.
For instance, say a ULIP fund invests in 1000 securities whose aggregate market value is Rs.50,000. In this case, the NAV would be Rs.50. Now, if you pay a premium of Rs.5 lakhs, you would get 10,000 units in the fund.
What is the good time to invest in ULIPs?
There is no right time to invest in ULIP. You can invest in the plan anytime. In fact, it is recommended that you invest in ULIPs as early as possible so that you can choose a long policy tenure over which your investments can grow considerably. Moreover, when you invest early, you can invest in affordable amounts and still amass a considerable corpus with the power of compounding.
How to maximise ULIP returns?
To maximise your ULIP returns, monitor your investments closely. Invest in equity-oriented funds that have a high return potential. If the equity market turns volatile, switch your investment to debt funds to safeguard the returns that you have earned.
Also, avoid partial withdrawals to allow your fund to grow and continue the premium payment over the chosen tenure.
How is the fund value calculated in a ULIP plan?
The fund value is calculated as follows –
Fund value = premium paid – charges deducted + returns earned
This means that the fund value represents the growth of your invested premium after deducting the applicable charges.
For how long is the premium locked in under ULIP?
The lock-in period in ULIP is five years.
Is the maturity amount under ULIPs taxable?
ULIP policy is issued before 1 Feb 2021, maturity amount will be tax free in the hands of recipient if policy is satisfying conditions mentioned under Section 10(10D) of Income Tax Act.
For policies issued on or after 1 February 2021:
If the aggregate premium of all unit-linked plans that you have is equal to or less than Rs.2.5 lakhs, maturity amount will be tax free in the hands of recipient if policy is satisfying conditions mentioned under Section 10(10D) of Income Tax Act.
Similarly, if the aggregate premium is more than Rs.2.5 lakhs, the maturity proceeds would attract capital gains tax. This tax would be applicable as follows –
If you had invested in equity funds, returns up to Rs.1 lakh would be tax-free. If, however, the returns exceed Rs.1 lakh, the excess would be taxed at 10% for long term capital gain.
If you had invested in debt funds, the returns would be taxed at 20% with the benefit of indexation.
Disclaimer:
In this policy, the investment risk in investment portfolio is borne by the policyholder. Investment in ULIPs is subject to risks associated with the capital markets. The policy holder is solely responsible for his/her decisions while investing in ULIPs.The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale.
Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Ltd., Regd. office Address: Bajaj Allianz House, Airport Road, Yerawada, Pune - 411006, Reg. No.: 116, CIN: U66010PN2001PLC015959, Call us on toll free No.: 1800 209 7272, Mail us: customercare@bajajallianz.co.in, Fax No: 02066026789
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility.
Conditions Apply – The guaranteed benefits are dependent on the purchase price & annuity option chosen. For more details please refer to sales brochure.

output.final_text:
Unit Linked Insurance Plan (ULIP)
What is ULIP?
What do you get when you combined life insurance and market-linked investments?
You get a Unit Linked Insurance Plan (ULIP).
Short for Unit Linked Insurance Plans, ULIPs are new-age life insurance policies that offer you the dual benefits of insurance coverage and market-linked returns. With ULIPs, you can create wealth by investing in market-linked instruments, all the while enjoying protection through life insurance. In the case of premature death, the plan pays death benefit that helps your family deal with financial loss.
What Is a ULIP Plan?
A ULIP plan is a life insurance policy wherein your premiums are invested in the market-linked funds of your choice. With ULIPs you can plan for different life goals like child’s education, buying a property, retirement planning etc. and create a suitable corpus for your financial goals. The plan gives you insurance protection and also allows you to earn returns based on market performance of the funds.
ULIP Plan: Benefits You Should Know
1. Market Linked Returns
ULIPs are the only life insurance plans that allow you to invest in the capital markets and get returns linked to their performance. The capital market has the potential to offer exponential market-linked returns on your investments. You can harness this potential with the help of ULIPs. 2. Dual Benefit of Investment and Insurance
ULIPs also give you the security of insurance coverage. So, while you invest money in ULIPs to create a corpus for your financial needs, you can be assured that your family’s financial needs will be taken care of in your absence. 3. Flexibility
ULIP plans are a flexible investment option. It offers flexibility, subject to plan’s terms & conditions, to-
● Switch between investment funds based on market outlook ● Make partial withdrawals ● Do a top-up to invest an additional sum of money ● Do a premium redirection as per the change of financial goals 4. Level Paying Premiums
Under ULIPs, the premium does not change after the policy starts. You pay a level premium throughout the premium payment tenure. This helps you plan for your investment beforehand without any surprises. Moreover, you can also pay a single premium if you want to invest once and get covered for a longer period of time. 5. Even Distribution of Charges
The charges associated with ULIPs are not deducted once. They are distributed over the policy tenure so that you don’t feel the pinch at once. . 6. Tax Benefits
You can save taxes with ULIPs. Here’s how –
● Premium paid is allowed as a deduction under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961 ● The death benefit is tax-free ● Partial withdrawals are not taxed, subject to satisfaction of conditions mentioned under Section 10(10D) of Income Tax Act. ● Surrender value is tax-free under Section 10(10D), subject to satisfaction of conditions mentioned therein. Also note that if policy is surrendered before 5 years from the date of commencement of policy, any deduction claimed towards premium under Section 80C will be treated as Income in the year of surrender of such ULIP policy. ● The maturity benefit is also tax-free under Section 10(10D) provided the premium paid was up to Rs.2.5 lakhs, and it was not more than 10% of the sum insured. ● Tax benefits are subject to change as per tax laws, consult your tax consultant for details
In case of high-value ULIP (Premium above Rs. 2.50 lakhs and issued after 1 Feb 2021), the tax treatment will be governed basis whether such policy qualifies to be unit of an ‘equity-oriented fund’. If the fund invests minimum 65% of the total proceeds in equity shares of listed domestic company or 90% of total proceeds in units of another listed fund, which in-turn invest 90% of proceeds in equity shares of listed domestic company, in such a case it shall be treated as equity-oriented fund. Such investment thresholds are required to be satisfied throughout the term of such insurance policy. Thus, if the above investment thresholds are satisfied, the high-value ULIP shall be treated as unit of equity-oriented fund. Else, ULIP shall be treated as unit of debt- oriented fund. The applicable tax rates under Income Tax Act basis the classification of capital gains between long-term and short-term is as follows.
Particulars Equity oriented ULIP Debt oriented ULIP
LTCG 10% (without indexation) u/s. 112A
20% (without indexation) u/s. 112
STCG 15% u/s. 111A As per applicable Slab rates
Holding period
1 year 3 years
7. High Liquidity
ULIPs are liquid plans as they allow you to make partial withdrawals after the lock-in period is over, subject to policy terms & conditions. Such withdrawals are tax-free subject to provisions stated in the Income Tax Act, 1961 and allow you to meet your financial needs without surrendering the policy.
How Do ULIPs Work?
ULIPs are different from other life insurance policies. Under these plans, you know exactly how much premium is being invested, how, and the returns that you are earning from opted Fund(s). So, let’s see how ULIPs work.
● When you buy a ULIP, you pay a premium for the plan. You also choose the sum assured (as a multiple of the premium), the policy term, the premium payment term and the fund into which you want to invest. ● You can choose market-linked funds such as equity, debt, liquid or combination of these funds to invest in your premium. ● If you have a high-risk appetite, you can choose to invest in equity funds. On the other hand, if you aren't willing to take too much of a risk, you can put your money in a debt fund. To enjoy the benefits of returns without high risks, people also prefer investing in a combination of both equity and debt funds. ● Once the selection is made, the insurance company will deduct the relevant charges from the premium. ● The premium, net of charges, also called allocated premium, is invested in the selected fund(s) ● The relevant charges are deducted as and when they accrue subject to terms and conditions of the policy ● The fund value is the value of your invested premium, with returns ● The death benefit is guaranteed* to be higher than the sum assured or the fund value as per the terms and conditions of the policy ● On maturity, however, you receive the fund value. ULIPs also allow the settlement option, subject to terms and conditions on maturity. Under this option, you can withdraw your maturity amount in instalments over five years rather than in one lump sum subject to policy terms & conditions ● During the plan term, ULIPs allow top-ups, switching, partial withdrawals and premium redirections on fulfillment of policy terms & conditions, so that you can manage your investment easily.
How to Choose the suitable ULIP Plan for you?
As mentioned earlier, there are different types of ULIP plans available in the market. So, how do you choose the suitable plan?
The answer is simple, through a fact-finding analysis.
To choose the right plan, do the following –
● Evaluate your financial needs and goals. Then, depending on them, choose a suitable ULIP. For instance, if you want to plan for your child’s future, you can opt for child ULIPs. On the other hand, if you want to plan for your retirement, go for pension ULIPs. ● Assess your coverage needs. After selecting the type of ULIP, choose the sum assured that would be optimal to cover your financial needs. ● Estimate the premium. Decide on the premium that you can comfortably pay towards the policy to create the desired corpus. Also, assess for how long you can pay the premium. ● Choose the term of the policy based on your financial goals ● Choose the fund(s) depending on your risk profile and investment strategy ● Check the charges. Opt for plans that have lower charges so that a maximum part of your premium is invested ● Check the plan benefits to know exactly what you would receive on death or maturity
Are ULIPs Good for Me?
Since ULIPs do not guarantee returns and are exposed to market-related risks, many of you might wonder if they are good for you or not. To find the answer to this question, ask yourself the following questions instead –
1. Why Are You Investing?
ULIPs have been designed to help you meet your financial goals. So, before you invest in a goal- oriented plan, make sure you outline your financial goals and identify the plan that will help you achieve your life goals. 2. What Kind of Charges Do I Need to Pay?
Since ULIPs offer individuals both insurance and investment, there are charges that come with investing in such a plan. Before you decide to put your money into a ULIP, make sure you educate yourself on the kind of charges that each insurance provider levies on their ULIPs. 3. How Do I Decide My Insurance Cover?
It's important to remember that along with investment opportunities, ULIPs also offer life cover. They are first and foremost insurance products, so you need to choose adequate life cover for yourself to safeguard your loved one's financial future. If you opt for the maximum sum assured offered by an insurer as per the terms & conditions of the plan, your family will be provided with adequate financial backing in the event that something happens to you. Now that you have a better understanding of what is a ULIP plan, you can safely decide how and where you need to invest your money. If you're ready to safeguard your financial future, browse through different ULIP Plans that suit your needs.
Who Should Invest in ULIP Policy?
People with Varying Risk Appetite
ULIPs offer different fund options as per the risk appetite of the individual. There is a choice of funds for all types of individuals- from high-risk takers to risk-averse individuals that can help achieve their life goals.
People who Want to Closely Monitor their Investments:
A Unit Linked Insurance Plan allows you to closely monitor your investment portfolio. ULIPs also allow you to switch your funds to re-adjust the capital allocation with varying risk profiles as per policy terms and conditions.
People with Medium to Long Term Investment Horizon
ULIP is a suitable investment option for you if you are ready to stay invested for a relatively long period.
Invest across all Stages of Life
ULIPs offer different plans which can be selected by an investor depending upon the life stage he is currently in. ULIPs can protect your family against financial liabilities now depending on your life goal.
Types of ULIP Plans
As mentioned earlier, there are different types of ULIP plans. Let’s have a look at these types –
● Types of ULIPs based on the plan structure Depending on the type of plan and its benefit structure is, ULIPs are categorised into the following types –
o Endowment ULIPs
These are one of the most popular ULIP plans that help you maximise your market- linked wealth and grow a corpus for any financial goal. The plans are simple ULIPs with a guaranteed* benefit on death and the fund value payable on maturity. o Pension ULIPs
Pension ULIPs are specifically designed plans meant to create a retirement corpus. You pay premiums during the policy tenure which get accumulated into a retirement corpus. In the case of death, a death benefit is paid. However, when the plan matures, you can withdraw up to a certain limit of the accumulated corpus in cash. This is called the commutation of pension. The remaining corpus would be used to buy annuities from insurance company. o Whole-life ULIPs
Whole-life ULIPs are like endowment ULIPs with the only difference being that they run till 99 or 100 years of age. The premium payment, however, is limited up to a specific age, and you can enjoy lifelong coverage. Fund Option Under ULIPs
As mentioned earlier, there are three primary types of investment funds under ULIPs. These are as follows –
● Equity funds Equity funds are those that invest a major portion of their portfolio into equity stocks and related securities. These funds have a high equity exposure and are, thus, high-risk investment options with a potential for high returns too. ● Debt funds Debt funds invest a major proportion of their portfolio into debt instruments. Debt instruments are those that carry a fixed rate of interest. As such, debt instruments are not volatile. Debt funds, thus, are low-risk funds that offer low yet stable returns. ● Hybrid or balanced funds Hybrid funds or balanced funds are those that combine both equity and debt. The portfolio of these funds invests in equity and debt instruments. The high-risk nature of equity is stabilized by the low-risk debt component. Balanced funds, offer investors moderate returns at moderate risks.
Charges under ULIPs
There are different types of charges that are levied under ULIP plans. These charges are as follows –
● Premium allocation charge
This is an upfront charge which is deducted from the premium upfront before allocating in opted fund(s). ● Policy administration charge The charge is levied on monthly basis, for administering and servicing your policy. ● Fund management charge This charge is applied to manage your fund. Fund management charge depends on the type of fund that you choose. ● Mortality charge A mortality charge is a charge deducted to cover the cost of risk coverage that the plan provides. In simple terms, it is the cost of providing life insurance coverage on the plan. The mortality charge depends on your age and sum assured. Moreover, it is levied till the sum assured is lower than the fund value subject to terms and conditions of the policy. Once the sum assured exceeds the fund value, the mortality charge is not applicable. ● Surrender or discontinuance charges This charge is levied if you surrender your policy and charges applicable are as per policy terms and conditions. ● Rider charge If you choose a rider with your ULIP for additional coverage, you will be charged additional cost of insurance / premium. ● Miscellaneous charge The miscellaneous charge includes other charges associated with the policy like the charge of issuing a duplicate policy document, any other type of servicing charge, etc.
Important ULIP terms that you should know
When investing in ULIPs, you should understand the technical aspects of the plan. As such, to help you, here are some important ULIP terms that you should know about –
● Lock-in A ULIP policy has a lock-in period which means that the money that you invest stays invested for the period. The lock-in period is five years, during which you cannot make withdrawals. \ ● Switching Switching means changing between the investment funds. You can change the selected funds as and when you need them through switching. For instance, if you invest in an equity fund at inception, and then you want to move your investment to a debt fund, you can switch from equity to debt subject to policy terms & conditions. ULIP plans allow free switching facility subject to policy terms and conditions up to a specified number of times. Once you exceed the free switches, charges might apply. ● Partial withdrawals Partial withdrawals mean withdrawing funds from your ULIP after lock in period subject to policy terms and conditions. ● Premium redirection Premium redirection means redirecting subsequent premiums to another investment fund than what you chose to invest in. For instance, you choose equity initially and then redirect your future premiums to debt fund from the next year. ● Top-up premium
A top-up premium is an additional premium that you can pay towards a ULIP plan over and above the actual premium that you are paying. Top-up helps you invest more and also enjoy an additional sum assured. ● Settlement Option On maturity, you can choose to take the maturity benefit in instalments rather than in one lump sum. When you do so, it is called the settlement option. You can withdraw in instalments over a period of five years. This helps you stay invested for longer to enjoy better returns.
What is ULIP Calculator?
A calculator / Benefit Illustration is a tool that tells you the expected corpus you can create from the plan after considering the following factors –
● Investment return at 8% and 4% return from the selected fund(s) ● The amount of premium that you invest ● Your age (for determining the mortality charge) ● The sum assured chosen (for determining the mortality charge) ● The policy term ● The premium paying term and frequency ● Additional riders if chosen (for determining the rider charge) ● The applicable charges under the plan ● The death benefit ● The maturity benefit
How Is ULIP Plan Structured?
The premium under ULIP is collected from different policyholders and pooled into a collective corpus. The corpus is created for each type of fund that the ULIP plan offers.
After the corpus is created, it is allocated to different types of market-linked instruments depending on the objective of the fund. For instance, in the case of equity funds, the corpus is invested in the equity stocks of different companies and other equity securities.
The portfolio of every fund is managed by experienced fund managers who try and maximise returns and manage risks.
Thereafter, depending on how the securities perform, the fund grows or falls. As an investor, you get returns if the fund grows or lose if the fund falls.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
1. What is the meaning of ULIP? How does the plan work?
ULIP is an investment-oriented life insurance policy which provides life insurance coverage and investment returns. The premium that you pay towards the ULIP is invested in a market-linked fund that you choose after deducting charges.
In the case of death, the plan usually pays higher than the sum assured or the available fund value. On maturity, however, the available fund value is paid. This is subject to terms and conditions of the policy. ULIPs have a lock-in period of five years. After this period is over, you can make partial withdrawals. Switching and premium redirection are also allowed, wherein you can change your investment funds. Above benefits can be utilised subject to policy terms & conditions. 2. What are the things that should be remembered while investing in ULIPs?
You should remember the following when investing in a ULIP –
o There would be different types of charges associated with the plan o The returns would not be guaranteed. They would depend on the market performance
of the funds your premium is invested in o There would be a lock-in period of five years o If you invest more than Rs.2.5 lakhs in new ULIP policy/polices which are issued on or
after 1 February 2021, the maturity benefit would be taxable as per tax laws. 3. Do ULIPs make a suitable investment?
Yes, ULIP may be considered as a preferred investment because of the following reasons –
o It allows dual benefits of insurance and investment o It allows flexibility in the form of top-ups, switching of funds, premium redirection and
partial withdrawals subject to policy terms & conditions. o You can choose from the different types of ULIPs to find a suitable plan depending on
your financial goals o You can enjoy tax benefits on your investment and also on the returns subject to
provisions stated in the Income Tax Act, 1961. 4. How much premium is allocated towards the funds to buy units?
The premium, net of charges, is used to purchase units. The charges include the premium allocation charge, which is deducted upfront. Thereafter, other charges are deducted on a monthly or daily basis. For example: If the allocation charge is 5% then 95% of the premium that you pay would be invested in the fund that you choose. 5. Is it possible to cancel the policy and get a refund after buying it?
Yes, there’s a free-look period available under the ULIP policy which is for 15 days and in some cases of buying the policy online, the period is 30 days. During this period, you can cancel the plan if you are not satisfied with it. The refund amount is as per the terms and conditions of the policy. 6. What is the lock-in tenure under ULIP?
The minimum lock-in period for ULIP is five years. 7. What is the meaning of NAV in ULIPs?
The Net Asset Value (NAV) is the per unit cost of a fund. In simpler terms, the funds of a ULIP plan invest in different market-linked instruments. The aggregate value of such investment divided by the number of units gives the NAV. When you pay the premium, the number of units that you get depends on the NAV on the day of premium allocation. 8. What is the good time to invest in ULIPs?
There is no right time to invest in ULIP. You can invest in the plan anytime. In fact, you may consider investing in ULIPs as early as possible so that you can choose a long policy tenure over which your investments can grow considerably. Moreover, when you invest early, you can invest in affordable amounts and still amass a considerable corpus with the power of compounding. 9. How is the fund value calculated in a ULIP plan?
Fund value represents the growth of your invested premium after deducting the applicable charges. 10. Is the maturity amount under ULIPs taxable?
ULIP policy is issued before 1 Feb 2021, maturity amount will be tax free in the hands of recipient if policy is satisfying conditions mentioned under Section 10(10D) of Income Tax Act. For policies issued on or after 1 February 2021: If the aggregate premium of all unit-linked plans that you have is equal to or less than Rs.2.5 lakhs, maturity amount will be tax free in the hands of recipient if policy is satisfying conditions mentioned under Section 10(10D) of Income Tax Act. Similarly, if the aggregate premium is more than Rs.2.5 lakhs, the maturity proceeds would attract capital gains tax. This tax would be applicable as follows –
o If you had invested in equity funds, returns up to Rs.1 lakh would be tax-free. If,
however, the returns exceed Rs.1 lakh, the excess would be taxed at 10% for long term capital gain. o If you had invested in debt funds, the returns would be taxed at 20% with the benefit of
indexation.
